blocks.sol: The hypotenuse is given and one leg is missing, so subtract instead of add. Square the hypotenuse and pull out the known leg. $$b^2 = 17^2 - 8^2 = 289 - 64 = 225$$ Take the square root, \(b = \sqrt{225} = 15\). The missing leg is \(\boxed{15}\).

blocks.x: Whole-number right triangles. A few right triangles have whole numbers on all three sides, and they turn up so often it pays to know them: 3-4-5, 5-12-13, and 8-15-17. Any multiple of one works too, since scaling all three sides keeps the relationship, so 6-8-10 and 9-12-15 are just the 3-4-5 triangle grown larger. Spotting a triple lets you skip the arithmetic and read the answer straight off. The figure above shows two of them.
In the diagram above is a right triangle with legs \(12\) and \(16\). Find the hypotenuse. Give the number of units.
hints: The Pythagorean Theorem says \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\). Square both legs and add to get \(c^2\), then take the square root.
Look closer at \(12\) and \(16\). They are \(4\) times \(3\) and \(4\), so this is a 3-4-5 triangle scaled up by \(4\).
blocks.sol: By the Pythagorean Theorem, add the squares of the legs to get the square of the hypotenuse. $$12^2 + 16^2 = 144 + 256 = 400$$ So \(c = \sqrt{400} = 20\). You could also spot that \(12\) and \(16\) are \(4\) times \(3\) and \(4\). This is a 3-4-5 triangle scaled by \(4\), so the hypotenuse is \(4 \times 5 = 20\). The hypotenuse is \(\boxed{20}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with legs \(8\) and \(15\). Find the hypotenuse. Give the number of units.
hints: The hypotenuse squared equals the sum of the squares of the legs, so \(c^2 = 8^2 + 15^2\).
Add \(64 + 225\), then take the square root of that total.
blocks.sol: By the Pythagorean Theorem, the square of the hypotenuse is the sum of the squares of the legs. $$c^2 = 8^2 + 15^2 = 64 + 225 = 289$$ Take the square root, \(c = \sqrt{289} = 17\). This is the 8-15-17 triple. The hypotenuse is \(\boxed{17}\).

blocks.x: Reaching distances you cannot measure. The real power of the theorem is that it hands you a length you could never stretch a tape across. A ladder leans against a wall, a wire runs between the tops of two poles, a straight path cuts across a park from one corner to the opposite one. In every case the slanted distance is the hypotenuse of a right triangle, and its two legs are things you can actually measure. Walk along the ground to get one leg, look up the wall or the pole to get the other, and the theorem does the rest. You never climb the ladder or string the wire to find out how long it is. You measure the two easy sides on the ground and up the side, square them, add, and take the square root. \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\) turns two short measurements you can reach into one long one you cannot.
In the diagram above, a ladder leans against a wall. Its foot sits \(7\) feet from the wall, and the top reaches \(24\) feet up the wall. How long is the ladder? Give the number of units.
hints: The wall and the ground meet at a right angle, so the ladder, the ground, and the wall form a right triangle. The ladder leans across from the right angle, so it is the hypotenuse. The two legs are the \(7\) feet along the ground and the \(24\) feet up the wall.
By the Pythagorean Theorem, square each leg and add them to get the square of the ladder's length, \(c^2 = 7^2 + 24^2\). Work that sum out, then take the square root to get \(c\).
blocks.sol: The ladder is the hypotenuse, the side reaching across from the right angle where the wall meets the ground. The legs are \(7\) and \(24\), so the Pythagorean Theorem gives $$c^2 = 7^2 + 24^2 = 49 + 576 = 625.$$ Taking the square root, \(c = \sqrt{625} = 25\). This is the \(7\)-\(24\)-\(25\) right triangle, another whole-number set worth recognizing on sight. The ladder is \(\boxed{25}\) feet long.

blocks.x: In the diagram above, the tops of two poles are joined by a straight wire. The tops differ in height by \(20\) feet, and the poles stand \(21\) feet apart. Find the length of the wire. Give the number of units.
hints: The wire, the horizontal gap, and the height difference form a right triangle, with the wire as the hypotenuse. Which two lengths are the legs?
By the Pythagorean Theorem, square the two legs and add them, then take the square root to get the wire.
blocks.sol: The wire is the hypotenuse of a right triangle whose legs are the height difference \(20\) and the horizontal gap \(21\). By the Pythagorean Theorem, $$c^2 = 20^2 + 21^2 = 400 + 441 = 841.$$ Taking the square root, \(c = \sqrt{841} = \boxed{29}\). The wire is \(29\) feet long.

blocks.x: In the diagram above, two points sit \(9\) units apart horizontally and \(40\) units apart vertically. The straight line between them is the hypotenuse of a right triangle whose legs are those two gaps. Find the straight distance between the points. Give the number of units.
hints: The horizontal and vertical gaps are the two legs, and the straight distance is the hypotenuse \(c\). Use \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\) with the legs \(9\) and \(40\).
Square each leg and add. \(9^2 = 81\) and \(40^2 = 1600\), so \(c^2 = 1681\). Now take the square root.
blocks.sol: The straight distance is the hypotenuse, with the horizontal and vertical gaps as the legs. By the Pythagorean Theorem, $$c^2 = 9^2 + 40^2 = 81 + 1600 = 1681.$$ Taking the square root, \(c = \sqrt{1681} = 41\). So the distance is \(\boxed{41}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above, a rectangle has a diagonal of length \(26\) and one side of length \(10\). Find the length of the other side. Give the number of units.
hints: The diagonal cuts the rectangle into two right triangles. In each one the two sides of the rectangle are the legs and the diagonal is the hypotenuse.
You know the hypotenuse and one leg, so use \(b^2 = c^2 - a^2\) to find the missing leg.
blocks.sol: The diagonal is the hypotenuse of a right triangle whose legs are the two sides of the rectangle. To find a missing leg you subtract the squares and take the square root, $$\sqrt{26^2 - 10^2} = \sqrt{676 - 100} = \sqrt{576} = 24.$$ So the other side is \(\boxed{24}\).

blocks.label: Fun fact
blocks.x: Though it carries Pythagoras's name, this relationship was known to the Babylonians more than a thousand years before he was born. Clay tablets show they were already working with whole-number right triangles. Today there are over four hundred different proofs of the theorem, and one of them was worked out by James Garfield, a future president of the United States, while he was serving in Congress.
In the diagram above is a right triangle with legs \(35\) and \(12\). Find the hypotenuse. Give the number of units.
hints: The Pythagorean Theorem says the squares of the two legs add up to the square of the hypotenuse, \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\). Square each leg and add.
You should have \(35^2 + 12^2 = 1369\). The hypotenuse is the square root of that. Try to spot which whole number squares to \(1369\).
blocks.sol: By the Pythagorean Theorem, the squares of the legs add to the square of the hypotenuse. $$c^2 = 35^2 + 12^2 = 1225 + 144 = 1369$$ Take the square root, and \(c = \sqrt{1369} = 37\). This is the 12-35-37 right triangle. The hypotenuse is \(\boxed{37}\).

blocks.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with hypotenuse \(25\) and one leg \(20\). The other leg is unknown. Find the area of the triangle. Give the number of square units.
hints: To find the area you need a base and a height, and the two legs work perfectly. But you only know one leg, so use the Pythagorean Theorem first to get the missing one.
Once you have both legs, the area of a right triangle is half the product of the legs, since the legs are the base and height.
blocks.sol: This is two steps. First find the missing leg with the Pythagorean Theorem. The hypotenuse is \(25\) and one leg is \(20\), so $$b = \sqrt{25^2 - 20^2} = \sqrt{625 - 400} = \sqrt{225} = 15.$$ Now the two legs are \(20\) and \(15\), and in a right triangle the legs are the base and height. So the area is $$\frac{1}{2}\times 20 \times 15 = \boxed{150}.$$

blocks.x: The Pythagorean toolkit. Pull the lesson together. In a right triangle the legs meet at the right angle and the hypotenuse lies opposite it, the longest side. The theorem \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\) is exact, and it holds because four copies of the triangle fill an \((a+b)\) square around a \(c\) square, forcing \(a^2 + b^2\) to equal \(c^2\). To find the hypotenuse, add the squares of the legs and take the root; to find a missing leg, subtract from the hypotenuse's square and take the root. Learn the triples 3-4-5, 5-12-13, and 8-15-17 and their multiples so you can spot them on sight. And whenever a right angle turns up in the world, a ladder, a wire between two poles, a distance across a map, the theorem hands you the slanted length you could not measure directly.

review.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with legs \(16\) and \(12\). Find the hypotenuse. Give the number of units.
hints: The hypotenuse is the side opposite the right angle. By the Pythagorean Theorem it equals \(\sqrt{16^2 + 12^2}\), so square each leg, add, then take the square root.
review.sol: The two legs are \(16\) and \(12\), so the hypotenuse is the square root of the sum of their squares.$$c = \sqrt{16^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{256 + 144} = \sqrt{400} = 20$$So the hypotenuse is \(\boxed{20}\).

review.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with legs \(24\) and \(7\). Find the hypotenuse. Give the number of units.
hints: The hypotenuse is the longest side, opposite the right angle. By the Pythagorean Theorem its square equals the sum of the squares of the two legs, so square \(24\) and \(7\), add them, then take the square root.
review.sol: The two legs form the right angle, so they are \(a\) and \(b\), and the hypotenuse is \(c\). By the Pythagorean Theorem, $$c^2 = 24^2 + 7^2 = 576 + 49 = 625.$$ Taking the square root, \(c = \sqrt{625} = \boxed{25}\). This is the 7-24-25 right triangle, another whole-number triple worth recognizing on sight.

review.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with legs \(21\) and \(20\). Find the hypotenuse. Give the number of units.
hints: The two shorter sides are the legs, so use \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\). Square each leg, add, then take the square root.
review.sol: By the Pythagorean Theorem, the hypotenuse is the square root of the sum of the squares of the legs. $$c = \sqrt{21^2 + 20^2} = \sqrt{441 + 400} = \sqrt{841}$$ Since \(29^2 = 841\), the hypotenuse is \(\boxed{29}\).

review.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with hypotenuse \(25\) and one leg \(24\). Find the other leg. Give the number of units.
hints: The missing side is a leg, so the theorem rearranges to \(b^2 = c^2 - a^2\). Subtract the square of the known leg from the square of the hypotenuse, then take the square root.
review.sol: The hypotenuse is the longest side, \(25\), and one leg is \(24\), so the missing leg \(b\) satisfies \(24^2 + b^2 = 25^2\). Subtracting to isolate \(b^2\),$$b^2 = 25^2 - 24^2 = 625 - 576 = 49.$$Take the square root, \(b = \sqrt{49} = \boxed{7}\). This is the 7-24-25 right triangle, one more of the whole-number triples worth recognizing on sight.

review.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with hypotenuse \(15\) and one leg \(9\). Find the missing leg. Give the number of units.
hints: You know the hypotenuse and one leg, so use the theorem in its subtraction form, \(b^2 = c^2 - a^2\). Square each length, subtract, then take the square root.
review.sol: Since we know the hypotenuse and want a leg, we subtract. By the Pythagorean Theorem, \(b^2 = c^2 - a^2\), so $$b^2 = 15^2 - 9^2 = 225 - 81 = 144.$$ Taking the square root, \(b = \sqrt{144} = 12\). The missing leg is \(\boxed{12}\).

review.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with hypotenuse \(41\) and one leg \(40\). Find the length of the other leg. Give the number of units.
hints: You know the hypotenuse and one leg, so this is a subtract-then-take-the-square-root problem. \(b^2 = c^2 - a^2\). Square both known sides and subtract.
review.sol: To find a missing leg, subtract the squares and take the square root. Here the hypotenuse is \(41\) and the known leg is \(40\), so$$b^2 = 41^2 - 40^2 = 1681 - 1600 = 81,$$and \(b = \sqrt{81} = 9\). The three sides \(9\), \(40\), \(41\) are a Pythagorean triple. The other leg is \(\boxed{9}\) units.

review.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with legs \(24\) and \(10\). Find the hypotenuse. Give the number of units.
hints: The Pythagorean Theorem says \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\), so square both legs and add them, then take the square root to get the hypotenuse.
review.sol: The hypotenuse is the longest side, opposite the right angle. By the Pythagorean Theorem, \(c^2 = 24^2 + 10^2\), so $$c = \sqrt{576 + 100} = \sqrt{676} = 26.$$ The hypotenuse is \(\boxed{26}\).

review.x: In the diagram above, a ladder leans against a wall. Its foot sits \(5\) feet from the wall and its top reaches \(12\) feet up. The ladder itself is the hypotenuse of the right triangle. How long is the ladder? Give the number of units.
hints: The wall and the ground are the two legs, and the ladder is the hypotenuse. By the Pythagorean Theorem the ladder's length squared equals \(5^2 + 12^2\). Add those, then take the square root.
review.sol: The wall and the ground meet at a right angle, so the ladder is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs \(5\) and \(12\). By the Pythagorean Theorem, $$c^2 = 5^2 + 12^2 = 25 + 144 = 169.$$ Taking the square root, \(c = \sqrt{169} = 13\). This is the \(5\text{-}12\text{-}13\) right triangle, one of the whole-number triples worth knowing by heart. The ladder is \(\boxed{13}\) feet long.

review.x: In the diagram above the two points are \(8\) apart horizontally and \(15\) apart vertically. The straight line joining them is the hypotenuse of a right triangle whose legs are those two gaps. Find the distance between the points. Give the number of units.
hints: The horizontal and vertical gaps are the two legs of a right triangle, and the distance you want is the hypotenuse. By the Pythagorean Theorem, add the squares of the legs and take the square root.
review.sol: The horizontal gap and the vertical gap are the legs of a right triangle, and the distance between the points is the hypotenuse. By the Pythagorean Theorem, add the squares of the legs and take the square root. $$\sqrt{8^2 + 15^2} = \sqrt{64 + 225} = \sqrt{289} = 17$$ So the distance between the points is \(\boxed{17}\) units. This is the 8-15-17 triple, one worth recognizing on sight.

review.x: In the diagram above, a wire runs from the top of one pole to the top of another. The tops differ in height by \(24\) feet, and the poles stand \(18\) feet apart. Find the length of the wire. Give the number of units.
hints: The wire is the hypotenuse of a right triangle whose legs are the horizontal gap and the height difference. Use \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\).
review.sol: The wire is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs \(18\) and \(24\), so by the Pythagorean Theorem $$18^2 + 24^2 = 324 + 576 = 900.$$ Take the square root, \(\sqrt{900} = 30\). The wire is \(\boxed{30}\) feet long.

review.x: The diagram above shows a rectangle with a diagonal of \(37\) and one side of \(12\). The diagonal cuts the rectangle into a right triangle, with the diagonal as the hypotenuse. Find the length of the other side. Give the number of units.
hints: The diagonal is the hypotenuse and the side of \(12\) is one leg, so use the Pythagorean Theorem to find the missing leg: square the diagonal, subtract the square of the known side, then take the square root.
review.sol: The diagonal is the hypotenuse, so the missing side is a leg. To find a leg, subtract the squares. $$b^2 = 37^2 - 12^2 = 1369 - 144 = 1225.$$ Take the square root. \(b = \sqrt{1225} = 35\). The other side is \(\boxed{35}\) units.

review.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with legs \(40\) and \(9\). Find the hypotenuse. Give the number of units.
hints: To find the hypotenuse, the Pythagorean Theorem says to add the squares of the two legs and take the square root.
review.sol: The hypotenuse squared equals the sum of the squares of the legs, so $$c^2 = 40^2 + 9^2 = 1600 + 81 = 1681.$$ Take the square root, \(c = \sqrt{1681} = 41\). The hypotenuse is \(\boxed{41}\).

review.x: In the diagram above is a right triangle with hypotenuse \(17\) and one leg \(15\). Find the area of the triangle. Give the number of square units.
hints: The two legs are the base and height, so you need the missing leg first. Use \(b^2 = c^2 - a^2\) to find it, then take half of base times height.
review.sol: You can't find the area yet, because you only have one leg. Get the other one from the theorem, subtracting to find a missing leg.$$b^2 = 17^2 - 15^2 = 289 - 225 = 64,\quad b = \sqrt{64} = 8$$Now the two legs, \(15\) and \(8\), are the base and height of the right triangle, so the area of a triangle is half of base times height.$$\tfrac{1}{2}\times 15 \times 8 = \boxed{60}$$The area is \(60\) square units.
